paragraphs.body: In the article "The Absolute Best Way to Cook Steak, According to So Many Tests," Ella Quittner compares different ways to cook a steak. She offers tricks, looks at six different methods of cooking, and grades the steak on three different criteria. First, she lists many tips on what to do with your steak. How do you season it, what do you do if you do not have a meat thermometer, and how long do you leave your steak out before cooking it? Next, Quittner analyzes six different methods to cook a steak: Stovetop only, stovetop to oven, stovetop to broiler, reverse sear, sous vide to sear, and grill. For every method, she gives you the amount of oil, the temperature of the appliance you are using, the amount of time you need to cook it, and how to enhance the flavor. Finally, Quittner categorizes the steak with three different criteria: Tenderness of meat, Overall ease and efficiency of the method, and the char of the meat. She concludes in her article that Sous vide to sear has the most tender meat, the stovetop is best in terms of overall ease and efficiency of the method, and the grill has the best char. New Orleans has some of the best food in the world. We have amazing restaurants and food trucks, and on a random Saturday night, at 2 am, you could go to a gas station and eat a hot plate of some of the best fried chicken and red beans and rice you have ever had. But to me, what makes the culture of food in New Orleans so great is that everyone has someone in their family who sets the standard for a meal. The best compliment you can hear if you make someone food in New Orleans is, "This is the second best dish I had only to that family member's meal." In my family, the person who sets the standard is my mama with her filet mignon. When I come home, step out of the car, and smell the air, and it is my mama's cooking, I know I am in for a good night. When I left home in New Orleans for college in Pittsburgh, I quickly realized my meals would not be the same. Cynthia Ozick asserts, "We often take for granted the very things that most deserve our gratitude." The Ozick point is that the thing most important to us is that we often don't recognize them. I was not aware of how special my mama's meals were until I was in the dorm room with no one to cook for me. After a few weeks of eating food at Pitt dining halls and having to carry around Tony's seasoning everywhere, I had enough. I went to Target and got an air fryer, a hotplate, and a crockpot. I have become obsessed with learning how to cook. I would go on the internet and look up recipes and test different combinations of seasoning, but when I had a question or needed advice, I would call my mama. One of my favorite meals is when my mama makes a filet mignon, so when I got one at the grocery store, my mom walked me through step by step the way she cooks it. I got better at cooking with each meal, and every time I would cook, I tasted a little bit of home. After about a month and a half of cooking, it became quite tedious, and it became a chore in my life. Making a good meal takes a lot of effort, and I did not have the energy to do that every day. I put a little less care into the food, and I tasted the difference. The only time I would have a passion for making a meal was when my friend would ask for some food. Thomas Keller himself writes, "A recipe has no soul. You as the cook must bring the soul to the recipe." In other words, Keller believes that if you do not have the enthusiasm to cook, then nothing you do can make the food taste good. The reason the food is so perfect in New Orleans is because it is made with soul. It can be the cafeteria lady or a gourmet chef in a 5-star restaurant. The thing that makes the meal is the fulfillment they get when someone enjoys their meal. There is no better representation of this than my mama. She would drop my brother and me off, go to work for 8 hours, bring us to practices, and then go back home. There had to be nights when she was exhausted and did not feel like cooking, but every night, she made sure my brother and I had a meal that was cooked with her soul. I always chuckle when people say that it is their mama or family member who makes the best food because there can only be one person who makes the best food. But when someone says that, they are not lying or embellishing their family members. They really do mean that because they see the sacrifice that goes into those meals, and that makes the food taste better. So when someone asks what my favorite food is? There is only one answer: my mama filet mignon. New Orleans has set my expectation of good food at a remarkably high bar, but I have come to understand that it is not the food I miss but the culture behind it. When I walk into a restaurant and take a bite of the food, I know I will feel the love it was made with. My mama showed and taught me what love is. I have learned that I am not obsessed with learning how to cook but with sharing that love through food with the people I care about.
